name: Igbo
description: The Igbo are a people of south-eastern Nigeria known for their egalitarian social philosophy ('Igbo enwe eze' — the Igbo have no king), entrepreneurial spirit, and rich artistic tradition. Their communities historically governed themselves through village assemblies and title societies rather than centralised kingdoms. The Igbo-Ukwu bronzes (9th century) demonstrate a mastery of lost-wax casting predating European contact. The diaspora produced major literary figures including Chinua Achebe, whose 'Things Fall Apart' made Igbo storytelling global.
cultural_highlights: Ozo title society — an earned prestige system where men of wealth and character ascend through titled grades, each with specific rights
Mmanwu masquerade — masked ancestral spirits that appear during festivals and funerals to enforce social order and entertain
Igbo-Ukwu bronzes — 9th-century ritual bronzes discovered in 1939, demonstrating sophisticated metallurgy 600 years before European influence
New Yam Festival (Iri Ji) — the most important annual festival, marking harvest and honouring the earth deity (Ala)
Chi personal spirit — every Igbo individual has a chi (personal spirit/soul) that accompanies them from birth

food_traditions.name: Ofe Onugbu (bitter leaf soup)
food_traditions.description: Bitter leaf soup with assorted meat and cocoyam — the flagship soup of Igbo cuisine

food_traditions.name: Nni ji (pounded yam)
food_traditions.description: Pounded yam eaten with soups — the preferred swallow for celebrations and hospitality

food_traditions.name: Ofe Oha
food_traditions.description: Ora leaf soup with cocoyam thickener — distinct from bitter leaf, this delicate herb soup is served at important occasions

food_traditions.name: Akara (bean cakes)
food_traditions.description: Deep-fried black-eyed pea fritters sold as street food and prepared as Sunday morning breakfast

music_traditions: Oje-aja (masquerade music) — flute, drum, and horn ensemble that accompanies mmanwu performances
Ekwe — a slit drum made from hollowed hardwood, used for community announcements and ceremonial music
Highlife — the guitar-driven genre that defines Igbo popular music; influenced contemporary Afrobeats
